# Discussion and Conclusions {#sec-discussion}

The preceding chapters have established a precise mathematical relationship between two inferential traditions that developed largely in isolation. Bayesian meta-analysis and Bayesian survey inference, when formulated under five exactness conditions, produce identical posterior distributions, identical sandwich variance estimators, and identical corrected credible intervals. This final chapter reflects on what that equivalence means, what it enables in practice, where it breaks down, and where the program might go next.


## Discussion: What the Equivalence Means {#sec-discussion-meaning}

The central result of this book is not an approximation, an analogy, or a heuristic correspondence. Under conditions (E1)--(E3) and (E5), the meta-analytic log-likelihood and the survey pseudo-log-likelihood differ by a constant that depends on no model parameters (Theorem 1, @sec-isomorphism). The constructive map $\mathcal{T}$ that sends cluster-specific sampling variances $\sigma^2_{e,j}$ to their within-cluster means $\bar{s}^2_j$ and survey weights $\tilde{w}_{ij}$ to unity is not a convenient simplification --- it is an exact algebraic identification. The two frameworks are the same mathematical object written in different notation.

This recognition carries an immediate and somewhat startling implication: any researcher who fits a compound-exchangeable or correlated-hierarchical-effects meta-analysis in Stan is already computing the survey pseudo-posterior. The inferential machinery of design-based survey statistics --- sandwich variance estimation, design effects, the Cholesky Calibration Correction --- applies to those posterior draws whether or not the analyst invokes it. The equivalence exists in the mathematics; the theorems in this book merely make it visible.

Several conceptual clarifications follow. First, the distinction between conditional and marginal design effect ratios, developed in @sec-conditional-marginal and formalized in Theorem 3, had no precedent in the meta-analytic literature. The decomposition $\mathrm{DER}^{\mathrm{cond}}_{\theta_j} = B_j \cdot \mathrm{DEFF}_j$, which separates the Bayesian shrinkage factor $B_j$ from the classical design effect, reveals that random-effects shrinkage and survey weighting interact at the level of individual cluster parameters --- a phenomenon that neither tradition had reason to articulate on its own. Second, the conservation law $\mathrm{DER}_\mu + \overline{\mathrm{DER}}^{\,\mathrm{cond}}_\theta = \mathrm{DEFF}$ (@sec-conservation-statement; see @fig-der-budget-conservation for the geometric trade-off) shows that the total design effect is partitioned, not dissipated, across the parameter hierarchy. A near-unity $\mathrm{DER}_\mu$ does not mean the design effect has vanished; it means the effect has been absorbed into the cluster-level parameters.

The practical consequence is direct. A meta-analyst who computes DER values for each parameter in the model can now determine exactly which credible intervals are trustworthy and which require sandwich-based correction. The @TannerSmithLipsey2015 empirical application illustrates this vividly: the overall mean $\mu$ sits comfortably in the calibrated tier ($\mathrm{DER}_\mu \approx 1.01$, T1), while the average cluster-level DER falls in the severe tier ($\overline{\mathrm{DER}}_\theta \approx 5.09$, T4). Without the equivalence framework, the analyst has no principled basis for knowing which parameters to trust and which to correct. With it, the answer is arithmetic.


## Implications for Practice {#sec-implications}

The equivalence results speak to three overlapping audiences, each of which gains something distinct.

**For meta-analysts**, the most immediate payoff is diagnostic. The DER, computed from posterior draws and the sandwich variance estimator as described in @sec-der-definitions, provides a scalar summary of model--design discrepancy for every parameter in the model. The tier classification from @LeeDER2026 --- T0 through T4, with boundaries at 0.95, 1.05, 2.0, and 4.0 --- gives the analyst a standardized severity scale for each parameter. The CCC (@sec-ccc-equiv) provides a post-MCMC correction that recalibrates posterior draws via a Cholesky transformation, requiring only matrix algebra on quantities already available from the Stan fit. No refitting is needed. The workflow is: fit once, diagnose with DER, correct where needed. In the @TannerSmithLipsey2015 data, the mean $\mathrm{DER}_\theta = 5.09$ (tier T4) implies that standard forest plot intervals should be widened by a factor of approximately $\sqrt{5.09} \approx 2.3$ --- a correction that is invisible to conventional meta-analytic output.

**For survey statisticians**, the equivalence provides a mirror image. The conservation law, $\mathrm{DER}_\mu + \overline{\mathrm{DER}}^{\,\mathrm{cond}}_\theta = \mathrm{DEFF}$, offers a decomposition of the classical design effect into components associated with different levels of the parameter hierarchy --- a partition that has no natural expression within the purely design-based tradition. The three-step gradient observed in the @TannerSmithLipsey2015 application (within-study moderators at $\sim 0.97$, between-study moderators at $\sim 1.11$, control covariates at $\sim 2.8$--$3.0$) demonstrates that design effects are not uniform across parameters but vary systematically with the covariate's position relative to the clustering structure. Survey practitioners working with hierarchical Bayesian models can use these results to anticipate which parameters will be most sensitive to design misspecification.

**For methodologists**, the deeper contribution is the demonstration that Calibrated Bayes [@Little2004; @Little2012] --- the principle that Bayesian credible intervals should be evaluated against their design-based frequentist coverage --- can be operationalized at the individual parameter level through the compute--classify--correct workflow. The DER is, in effect, a parameter-specific calibration diagnostic. When $\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k} \approx 1$, the model-based and design-based intervals agree; the posterior is already calibrated. When $\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k} \gg 1$, the model-based interval is too narrow; calibration requires the sandwich correction. The connection to @Rubin1984 is direct: the routine frequentist evaluation of Bayesian procedures that Rubin advocated is now embedded in the inferential pipeline at the individual-parameter level, at negligible computational cost relative to the MCMC sampling itself.


## Limitations {#sec-limitations}

Six limitations of the present work deserve explicit acknowledgment.

First, the equivalence between meta-analytic and survey frameworks is exact only under compound exchangeability. The extension to correlated hierarchical effects (CHE), discussed in @sec-che-ext, is conditional on the within-study heterogeneity variance $\omega^2$: the log-likelihoods coincide at each fixed $\omega^2$, but the marginal likelihood over $\omega^2$ introduces a term that depends on the framework-specific parameterization. Practical implications are minor when $\omega^2$ is well identified, but the analyst should be aware that the CHE equivalence is not fully unconditional.

Second, exactness condition (E2) requires that the sampling variances $\bar{s}_j^2$ be known. In practice, these are estimated from primary-study data and carry estimation uncertainty. When the estimated variances are substituted for the true values, the isomorphism holds approximately, with the approximation quality depending on the primary-study sample sizes. For meta-analyses with small primary studies, the (E2) violation may be consequential.

Third, condition (E5) restricts the framework to Gaussian Level 1 errors. This covers continuous effect sizes (standardized mean differences, Fisher-$z$ correlations, log-odds ratios) under the usual normal approximation, but excludes bounded, count, and binary outcomes. The extension to non-Gaussian likelihoods requires an estimating-equation approach, as demonstrated for the hurdle beta-binomial model in @LeeHBB2026, but the isomorphism theory of this book does not apply directly.

Fourth, the empirical illustration relies on a single dataset [@TannerSmithLipsey2015] with $J = 117$ studies and $N = 1{,}198$ effect sizes. While the theoretical results are exact under the stated conditions and do not require empirical validation, the practical performance of the DER/CCC pipeline --- including the conservation gap (8.7%) and the DER gradient across covariate types --- has been documented in only one dataset. Generalizability to other meta-analytic settings requires systematic simulation.

Fifth, the sandwich variance estimator is known to be liberal when the number of clusters $J$ is small, precisely the regime where Bayesian hierarchical models are most attractive. The regularity conditions of @sec-regularity ensure asymptotic validity as $J \to \infty$, but for meta-analyses with $J < 30$, the DER may be poorly estimated and the CCC correction may not achieve its nominal coverage targets. Small-sample corrections analogous to CR2 [@BellMcCaffrey2002; @Tipton2015] for the Bayesian DER pipeline remain undeveloped.

Sixth, the DER is computed conditional on a chosen working correlation $\rho_{\mathrm{work}}$. The DER values change --- sometimes substantially --- as $\rho_{\mathrm{work}}$ varies. Sensitivity analysis over a grid of $\rho$ values is recommended to assess the stability of the diagnostic conclusions, as discussed in the boundary analysis of @sec-boundary.


## Future Directions {#sec-future}

The limitations above suggest a natural research agenda, which I organize into six directions.

**Simulation studies.** A systematic Monte Carlo investigation varying $J$ (number of studies), $\bar{n}$ (average cluster size), $K$ (number of covariates), within-study covariate structures ($\gamma_k$ from 0 to 1), and degree of (E2) violation would establish the operating characteristics of the DER/CCC pipeline under realistic conditions. Of particular interest is the minimum $J$ required for adequate CCC performance and the behavior of the conservation gap as design imbalance increases.

**Non-Gaussian outcomes.** Extending the isomorphism to exponential-family likelihoods would cover binary, count, and bounded outcomes. The key question is whether the parameter-free constant $c$ in Theorem 1 survives when the log-likelihood involves a non-quadratic sufficient statistic. The estimating-equation approach of @LeeHBB2026 provides a practical template, but a formal isomorphism result for generalized linear mixed models remains open.

**Multi-way clustering.** Network meta-analysis introduces multiple clustering dimensions --- study and treatment-contrast --- that the two-level theory does not address. Extending the DER decomposition and conservation law to $L$-level hierarchies ($L \geq 3$) would require a system of conservation equations, one for each pair of adjacent levels, with level-specific shrinkage factors governing the redistribution dynamics.

**Adaptive DER thresholds.** The current five-tier classification uses fixed thresholds (0.95, 1.05, 2.0, 4.0). Treating the DER as a random variable with a posterior distribution --- induced by the posterior uncertainty in the sandwich components --- would allow probabilistic classification and data-adaptive threshold selection, potentially improving the sensitivity of the diagnostic in small-$J$ settings.

**Software implementation.** An integrated R package that wraps the compute--classify--correct workflow around Stan-based meta-analytic models would lower the barrier to adoption. The package would extract MCMC draws, compute the marginal and conditional sandwiches, evaluate the DER tier for each parameter, apply CCC where warranted, and produce diagnostic plots --- all as a post-processing step on a fitted Stan object.

**Bayesian model averaging.** The equivalence is established for a given working-covariance specification. Averaging over multiple working-covariance structures --- CE, HE, CHE with different $\omega^2$ --- using Bayesian model averaging weights could produce DER-calibrated posteriors that are robust to working-model uncertainty at a level the current framework does not address.


## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This book has established that Bayesian meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes and Bayesian survey inference under clustered sampling are, under five explicit conditions, not two methods but one, viewed from different disciplinary vantage points. The isomorphism map $\mathcal{T}$ identifies the likelihoods (Theorem 1), the sandwich variances (Theorem 2), the posteriors (Corollary 1), and the calibrated posteriors (Corollary 2) --- a chain visualized in @fig-likelihood-isomorphism through @fig-ccc-affine-map. The DER framework (Theorem 3) transfers the survey design-effect diagnostic tradition to meta-analysis, revealing a conservation law that constrains how the design-effect budget is allocated across parameters.

The Calibrated Bayes program of @Little2004 and @Little2012 --- Bayesian computation validated by frequentist coverage criteria --- is now available to meta-analysis at the individual-parameter level. The DER provides the diagnostic; the CCC provides the correction; the conservation law provides the structural constraint. The entire apparatus operates as a post-processing step on existing MCMC output, at negligible computational cost.

The practical message is concise. For any Bayesian correlated-effects meta-analysis fitted in Stan: extract the MCMC draws; compute the cluster-level sandwich variance; form the DER for each parameter; where the DER exceeds the T1 threshold, apply the Cholesky Calibration Correction. The result is a set of Bayesian credible intervals that honor both the hierarchical structure of the model and the design-based reality of within-study dependence --- the operational realization, for this class of models, of the Calibrated Bayes vision articulated by @Little2004 and @Rubin1984.

The open questions --- general working covariance structures, non-normal likelihoods, multi-level hierarchies, finite-sample corrections --- define a research program that bridges two communities with much to learn from each other. The foundation is in place. The dictionary has been written. The translation can begin.
